page.content: The data presented in Table 1 offers insights into dietary intake variations across different countries, specifically focusing on Denmark during the years 2000 to 2002. This period is representative of a national dietary survey conducted to assess individual-level dietary habits, which is crucial for understanding the nutritional status of the population.

lines.text: The data presented in Table 1 offers insights into dietary intake variations across different countries, specifically focusing on
Denmark during the years 2000 to 2002. This period is representative of a national dietary survey conducted to assess
individual-level dietary habits, which is crucial for understanding the nutritional status of the population.

page.content: The table includes metrics such as the "Lower limit" and "Upper limit," which delineate the range of dietary intake levels measured in the study. These parameters are essential for evaluating the adequacy of nutrient consumption among various age groups.

CountryYearDietary data levelN, ageMethodology usedReference
Denmark2000–2002National dietary survey, data at individua. levelF: 1,307; M: 1,156 25–64 years7-day pre-coded food record(25)
Finland2002National dietary survey (FINDIET), data at individual levelF: 1,095; M: 912 25–64 years48-h dietary recall(26)
Italy1994–1996National dietary survey, data at individual levelF: 682; M: 586 25–64 years7-day mixed survey technique(27)
Sweden1987–1990Cohort studies (Swedish Mammography Cohort (SMC) & Cohort of Swedish Men (COSM), data at group levelF: 37,854; M: 45,906 45–79 years96-item food frequency questionnaire (FFQ)(28)
United Kingdom2000–2001National dietary survey, data at individual levelF: 958; M: 76619–64 years7-day dietary record(29)

page.content: The inclusion of stratified data, denoted by the "age" column, allows for a more nuanced analysis of dietary patterns over time, emphasizing the importance of age-specific recommendations in nutritional guidelines.

page.content: Furthermore, the methodology used in gathering this data is vital for ensuring the reliability and validity of the findings, as outlined in the "Methodology used" column. Overall, this table serves as a critical reference point for researchers and policymakers aiming to enhance dietary standards and public health interventions.
